name: Spyre
classification_notes: Described as neo-classical, Spyre traces Roman forms through to transitional influences including Times New Roman and Plantin, with the Epoch subfamily introducing higher contrast and splayed serifs closer to old-style conventions.
summary: A neo-classical serif in two distinct subfamilies, one optimised for text and the other reintroducing higher contrast and traditional proportions drawn from engraving traditions.
description: Originating from a type design programme at Kingston School of Art, Spyre began as a revival of Trajan's Inscription before incorporating lowercase forms drawn from old-style and transitional references including Plantin and Times New Roman. The standard Spyre family is built for text, with sturdy serifs, balanced cap widths and a generous x-height, while Spyre Epoch subverts those conventions with heightened contrast, pointed and splayed serifs, and traditional narrower caps. Fourteen styles are offered across both subfamilies, with variable font support included.
foundry_blurb: British Standard Type describe Spyre as a neo-classical serif exploring how Roman letterforms have evolved into a modern transitional style. The family initiates a dialogue between the minimalist Crystal Goblet ideal and the intricate detailing of ancient engravings, with Spyre providing a text-oriented rigour and Spyre Epoch challenging contemporary norms through higher contrast, splayed serifs and traditional proportions.

name: Spyre Epoch
classification_notes: Described as neo-classical, Spyre Epoch sits firmly in the transitional serif tradition with heightened contrast, pointed and splayed serifs, and traditional cap widths that reference engraving-era models such as Times New Roman and Plantin.
summary: A neo-classical transitional serif with heightened contrast and pointed splayed serifs, developed as the more ornate counterpart to the plainer Spyre text family.
description: Originating from the type design programme at Kingston School of Art, Spyre Epoch pushes back against the rationalist tendencies of its sibling family by adopting traditional cap widths, lower x-height and sharper, more expressive serifs reminiscent of engraving-era models. Where Spyre reads as a clean contemporary text face, Epoch draws on Oldstyle and transitional references, including Times New Roman and Plantin, for a higher-contrast result suited to display and editorial work with classical flavour. The family ships in seven weights with a variable font also available.
foundry_blurb: British Standard Type describe Spyre Epoch as one half of a two-family dialogue between minimalist Crystal Goblet principles and intricate engraving-inspired detailing. The Epoch subfamily challenges contemporary norms through heightened contrast, pointed and splayed serifs, traditional cap widths, and a lower x-height, rooted in a revival of Trajan's Inscription that evolved through Oldstyle and transitional influences during study at Kingston School of Art.

name: BST Bazaine
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a contemporary grotesque, drawing from hand lettering on a Wim Crouwel poster; its high x-height, tight apertures and asymmetric junctures are characteristic of expressive grotesque design.
summary: A contemporary grotesque derived from Wim Crouwel's 1959 hand-lettered poster for a Jean René Bazaine exhibition at Eindhoven's Van Abbemuseum.
description: The letterforms originate from a single word on a Crouwel exhibition poster, extrapolated into a full family spanning seven weights from Thin to Bold. Compact boxy widths, a high x-height and tight apertures give the typeface a taut, distinctive silhouette, while asymmetric junctures introduce an uncommon tension across both display and text settings. OpenType features include unicase capitals, small caps and alternate forms for further typographic control.
foundry_blurb: British Standard Type describe BST Bazaine as a contemporary grotesque inspired by hand lettering on a Wim Crouwel poster made for a Jean René Bazaine exhibition at Eindhoven's Van Abbemuseum in 1959. The typeface takes its name and letterforms from that single word, developing its unusual contrast, compact forms, high x-height and asymmetric junctures into a family suited to both display and text use, with stylistic sets including unicase capitals, small caps and alternate forms.

name: BST Bazaine Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced grotesque with unusual contrast and asymmetric junctures, rooted in hand-lettered forms from a 1959 Wim Crouwel poster. The fixed-width construction combined with grotesque structure places it clearly in the grotesque monospace subcategory.
summary: A grotesque monospace drawn from Wim Crouwel's hand-lettered poster for a 1959 Bazaine exhibition, with tight apertures, asymmetric junctures and boxy proportions that give it a distinctive tension.
description: Rooted in the only surviving letterforms from a Wim Crouwel poster made for a Jean René Bazaine exhibition at Eindhoven's Van Abbemuseum, the typeface translates that hand-lettered source into a seven-weight monospace grotesque. Boxy widths, a high x-height and tight apertures create a compact, taut rhythm across both display and text settings. Stylistic sets include unicase capitals, small caps and alternate forms, extending its typographic range considerably.
foundry_blurb: British Standard Type describe BST Bazaine Mono as a contemporary grotesque monospace inspired by hand lettering on a Wim Crouwel poster created for a Jean René Bazaine exhibition at Eindhoven's Van Abbemuseum in 1959. The typeface takes its name and forms from the only existing lettered examples spelling the word 'Bazaine' on that poster, and is characterised by unusual contrast, asymmetric junctures, compact boxy widths and tight apertures. It works across display and text settings and includes OpenType features such as unicase capitals, small caps and alternate forms.

name: BST Ritma
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a contemporary humanist sans serif drawing on Johnston Sans, Gill Sans, Super Grotesk and Futura, with neo-grotesque design principles overlaid on a humanist structure.
summary: A contemporary humanist sans that blends Johnston and Gill Sans references with neo-grotesque proportions across seven weights.
description: Drawing on early twentieth-century British humanist typefaces including Johnston Sans and Gill Sans, as well as Super Grotesk and Futura, BST Ritma rejects classical monumental capital proportions in favour of neo-grotesque width principles. The result is a wide-bodied, even-textured family with a balanced x-height and dynamic rhythm across seven weights from Thin to Bold. Partner italics and a monospace sister family are listed as forthcoming.
foundry_blurb: British Standard Type describe BST Ritma as a contemporary humanist sans serif designed with an unusual rhythm, citing Johnston Sans, Gill Sans, Super Grotesk and Futura as reference points. They note it takes a modern approach to humanist typeface widths by rejecting capitalis monumentalis in favour of neo-grotesque design principles, and that future cuts including partner italics and a monospace family are in development.

name: BST Symbol Mono
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans serif with DIN and Bauhaus influences; the monospaced variant of BST Symbol fits the mono category given its fixed-width construction and geometric basis.
summary: A monospaced geometric sans from British Standard Type, drawing on DIN and Bauhaus principles across seven weights with distinctive bowl-filling behaviour at heavier settings.
description: Built on simplified geometric forms with minimal tapering at junctions, the design exhibits an intentional rawness as bowl-to-stem connections progressively fill towards black through the weight range. Seven weights from Thin to Bold are included, alongside stylistic sets featuring Space characters and stemless forms for varied typographic application. The family suits display and text settings where a disciplined, almost industrial geometry is the goal.
foundry_blurb: British Standard Type describe BST Symbol Mono as a geometric sans serif embodying a minimalist aesthetic with a distinct personality, drawing inspiration from DIN style typefaces and early Bauhaus design. They note its idiosyncratic interpolation behaviour, where bowl and stem connections fill toward black at heavier weights, resulting in an intentionally basic design that retains its soul. The family includes seven weights and multiple stylistic sets, with future cuts including an ultra-heavy weight in development.
